sections.heading: What are Deep Links and App Links?
sections.content: Deep links are URIs that navigate users directly to a specific screen or content inside your app, bypassing the home screen or app launcher. Android supports three types of deep links: standard deep links (custom URI schemes like myapp://), web deep links (HTTP/HTTPS URLs handled by your app alongside browsers), and Android App Links (verified HTTPS URLs that open exclusively in your app without a disambiguation dialog). App Links, introduced in Android 6.0 (API 23), require domain ownership verification via a Digital Asset Links JSON file hosted on your server. Deep links are essential for features like push notification navigation, email marketing CTAs, QR code scanning, web-to-app handoff, and cross-app navigation.

rows.disambiguation_dialog: No — if only one app handles the scheme
rows.security: Low — any app can register the same custom scheme
rows.use_case: Simple internal navigation; cross-app linking between your own apps

rows.disambiguation_dialog: Yes — user must choose between browser and app
rows.security: Medium — no verification; browser shown as fallback
rows.use_case: Universal links with browser fallback; basic web-to-app linking

rows.verification_required: Yes — Digital Asset Links JSON file on server
rows.disambiguation_dialog: No — opens directly in app; browser fallback if app not installed
rows.security: High — domain ownership verified by Android system
rows.use_case: Production web-to-app linking; push notification navigation; QR codes

sections.heading: Declaring Deep Links in the Manifest
items: Deep links are declared as intent filters on the target Activity in AndroidManifest.xml
Required intent filter actions: android.intent.action.VIEW
Required intent filter categories: android.intent.category.DEFAULT and android.intent.category.BROWSABLE
Custom scheme example: <data android:scheme="myapp" android:host="product" />
Web deep link example: <data android:scheme="https" android:host="example.com" android:pathPrefix="/product" />
Multiple <data> elements can be combined in one intent filter to handle multiple URL patterns
Use android:pathPrefix for prefix matching, android:path for exact matching, android:pathPattern for regex-style matching
Set android:autoVerify="true" on the intent filter to enable App Link verification (API 23+)

rows.description: URL scheme — use 'https' for App Links, custom string for custom scheme links
Domain name the link targets — must match your verified domain for App Links
Matches URLs whose path starts with the given string
Matches only the exact path specified
Matches paths using a limited regex — use .* for wildcards
Matches URLs whose path ends with the given string (API 31+)
Optional port number to match — omit for standard HTTP/HTTPS ports
Set to true on the intent filter to trigger App Link domain verification at install time

sections.heading: Android App Links — Domain Verification
sections.content: Android App Links require you to prove ownership of the domain by hosting a Digital Asset Links JSON file at a well-known location on your web server. The Android system fetches this file at app install time (and periodically thereafter) to verify the association between your domain and your app. Once verified, Android routes matching HTTPS URLs directly to your app without showing a disambiguation dialog. If verification fails, the links fall back to web deep link behavior — showing the disambiguation dialog. Verification requires the server to be publicly accessible and return the correct Content-Type header with the JSON file.

sections.heading: Setting Up Digital Asset Links
items: Host the file at: https://example.com/.well-known/assetlinks.json
The server must serve the file over HTTPS with Content-Type: application/json
File format: [{"relation": ["delegate_permission/common.handle_all_urls"], "target": {"namespace": "android_app", "package_name": "com.example.app", "sha256_cert_fingerprints": ["AA:BB:CC:..."]}}]
Get your SHA-256 fingerprint: keytool -list -v -keystore release.jks -alias key0 | grep SHA256
For debug builds use the debug keystore fingerprint: keytool -list -v -keystore ~/.android/debug.keystore -alias androiddebugkey -storepass android
Include fingerprints for both debug and release keystores during development
Add android:autoVerify="true" to the intent filter in the manifest
Test verification: adb shell pm get-app-links --user cur com.example.app — look for 'verified' status
Use Google's Digital Asset Links API to validate your file: https://digitalassetlinks.googleapis.com/v1/statements:list?source.web.site=https://example.com
Re-verification is triggered on app reinstall or system update — keep the assetlinks.json file live permanently

rows.meaning: Domain ownership confirmed — URLs open directly in app with no dialog
rows.check_command: adb shell pm get-app-links --user cur com.example.app

rows.meaning: Verification attempted but failed — falls back to disambiguation dialog behavior
rows.check_command: adb shell pm get-app-links --user cur com.example.app

rows.meaning: No verification attempted — android:autoVerify not set or no HTTPS intent filter
rows.check_command: adb shell pm get-app-links --user cur com.example.app

rows.meaning: User manually approved the app to handle the domain in system settings
rows.check_command: Settings > Apps > your app > Open by default

sections.heading: Handling Deep Links in an Activity
items: Retrieve the incoming Intent in onCreate(): val uri: Uri? = intent?.data
Also handle deep links delivered to a running Activity in onNewIntent(): override fun onNewIntent(intent: Intent) { super.onNewIntent(intent); handleDeepLink(intent.data) }
Parse path segments: uri?.pathSegments — returns a list of path components e.g. ['product', '123']
Parse query parameters: uri?.getQueryParameter("id") — returns the value of the 'id' query param
Parse the full URI string: uri?.toString() — for custom logic or regex matching
Always validate the URI before using it — never trust deep link parameters blindly
Sanitize path and query parameters — treat them as untrusted external input
Navigate to the correct screen using NavController: navController.navigate(deepLinkUri) or with explicit directions

sections.heading: Deep Links with Jetpack Navigation Component
sections.content: Jetpack Navigation Component has first-class support for deep links, making it the recommended way to handle deep link navigation in modern Android apps. You can declare deep link URIs directly in the navigation graph XML on the destination fragment or activity, and the Navigation Component automatically handles parsing the URI, extracting arguments, and navigating to the correct destination — including building the correct back stack. There are two types: explicit deep links (using PendingIntent for notifications) and implicit deep links (URI-based, handled from Intent data).

sections.heading: Declaring Deep Links in Navigation Graph
items: Add a <deepLink> element inside the destination in nav_graph.xml: <fragment android:id="@+id/productFragment"><deepLink app:uri="https://example.com/product/{productId}" /></fragment>
Use {placeholders} in the URI pattern to extract arguments — they map to the destination's arguments automatically
Arguments declared in <argument> tags are populated from the URI placeholders automatically by Navigation
Add the nav-graph to the manifest intent filter: <nav-graph android:value="@navigation/nav_graph" /> — replaces manually writing all intent filters
The NavHostFragment must be in the Activity that handles the deep link Intent
Handle implicit deep links: navController.handleDeepLink(intent) — call in Activity.onCreate() and onNewIntent()
Query parameters are also supported: app:uri="https://example.com/search?query={searchQuery}"
Multiple <deepLink> elements can be added to a single destination for different URI patterns

sections.heading: Explicit vs Implicit Deep Links in Navigation

rows.trigger: User taps a URL in a browser, email, or another app
rows.handling: Android routes the Intent to the Activity; NavController.handleDeepLink() navigates to the destination
rows.back_stack: Back stack built from the navigation graph — Back navigates up through parent destinations
rows.use_case: Web-to-app links, QR codes, universal links

rows.trigger: App creates a PendingIntent targeting a specific destination — used in notifications
rows.handling: NavDeepLinkBuilder creates a PendingIntent that launches the app and navigates to the destination
rows.back_stack: Synthetic back stack created — Back navigates up through specified parent destinations
rows.use_case: Push notification tap actions, widget tap actions, shortcuts

sections.heading: Explicit Deep Links for Notifications — NavDeepLinkBuilder
items: Use NavDeepLinkBuilder to create a PendingIntent that navigates to a specific destination when the notification is tapped

val pendingIntent = NavDeepLinkBuilder(context).setComponentName(MainActivity::class.java).setGraph(R.navigation.nav_graph).setDestination(R.id.productFragment).setArguments(bundleOf("productId" to "123")).createPendingIntent()
Pass the PendingIntent to NotificationCompat.Builder: .setContentIntent(pendingIntent)
NavDeepLinkBuilder automatically builds a synthetic back stack based on the navigation graph
The synthetic back stack means tapping Back from the destination goes to the parent screen, not to the previous app
setComponentName() specifies which Activity hosts the NavHostFragment
setArguments() passes a Bundle of arguments that map to the destination's declared arguments
Alternatively use: findNavController().createDeepLink().setDestination(R.id.productFragment).createPendingIntent()

rows.implementation: Use NavDeepLinkBuilder to create a PendingIntent targeting the specific destination; pass as notification's contentIntent
Use App Links (HTTPS) pointing to your domain; host assetlinks.json for verified direct-open behavior
Encode an HTTPS App Link URL in the QR code; scan with any reader and the verified App Link opens your app directly
Use custom URI scheme or explicit Intent with package name targeting; no server setup needed
Declare shortcut intents with deep link URIs in shortcuts.xml; use NavDeepLinkBuilder for dynamic shortcuts
Detect mobile browser on your website; redirect to App Link URL; falls back to web page if app not installed

sections.heading: Testing Deep Links
items: Test with adb: adb shell am start -W -a android.intent.action.VIEW -d "https://example.com/product/123" com.example.app
Test custom scheme: adb shell am start -W -a android.intent.action.VIEW -d "myapp://product/123" com.example.app
Verify App Link status: adb shell pm get-app-links --user cur com.example.app
Reset App Link verification: adb shell pm set-app-links --package com.example.app 0 all
Re-trigger verification: adb shell pm verify-app-links --re-verify com.example.app
Validate assetlinks.json online: https://digitalassetlinks.googleapis.com/v1/statements:list?source.web.site=https://example.com&relation=delegate_permission/common.handle_all_urls
Test in Android Studio: Run > Edit Configurations > Deep Link tab — enter URI and launch directly
Test back stack behavior — verify that Back navigates correctly after arriving via deep link

sections.heading: Common Deep Link Mistakes to Avoid

rows.mistake: Not handling deep links in onNewIntent()
rows.fix: Override onNewIntent() in your Activity and call handleDeepLink(intent.data) — deep links to a running app are delivered here, not onCreate()

rows.mistake: Forgetting android:category BROWSABLE in the intent filter
rows.fix: BROWSABLE is required for the system to route HTTP/HTTPS URLs to your app — omitting it means web links won't open your app
Always validate and sanitize URI path segments and query parameters — treat them as untrusted external input to prevent injection attacks
The file must be served at https://example.com/.well-known/assetlinks.json with correct Content-Type — redirects are not followed by the verifier

rows.mistake: Only including release keystore fingerprint in assetlinks.json
rows.fix: Include both debug and release keystore SHA-256 fingerprints in the assetlinks.json during development and testing

rows.mistake: Not setting android:launchMode for the deep link Activity
rows.fix: Use android:launchMode="singleTask" or handle onNewIntent() to prevent multiple instances of the Activity being created on repeated deep link taps

rows.mistake: Using custom URI schemes for public-facing links
rows.fix: Custom URI schemes (myapp://) offer no ownership verification — any app can intercept them; use verified App Links for public URLs

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: Deep links and App Links allow users to navigate directly to specific content in your app from the web, notifications, QR codes, and other apps. Use Android App Links (verified HTTPS URLs) for all public-facing links — they open your app directly without a disambiguation dialog and provide the strongest security through domain verification via the Digital Asset Links JSON file. Declare deep links in your navigation graph using <deepLink> elements and let the Navigation Component handle URI parsing, argument extraction, and back stack construction automatically. Use NavDeepLinkBuilder to create PendingIntents for notification tap actions with a proper synthetic back stack. Always handle deep links in both onCreate() and onNewIntent(), validate all incoming URI parameters, and test thoroughly with adb commands before release.
